creative.concept: Before/after commit message card: 'fix bug' (typed, 2 words) vs a full descriptive commit (spoken with Jot, 13 words). Developer audience hook — bad commits aren't laziness, they're typing friction. Doubles down on the HN developer base from June 16.

cards.title: Before/after: commit messages typed vs spoken
cards.use: Primary visual for LinkedIn, X, and Instagram. Export as 1080×1080 PNG. The '2 vs 13 words' contrast is the scroll-stopper; the symmetrical left/right panel layout invites direct comparison at a glance.

storyboard.title: The commit message your future self actually understands
storyboard.use: Instagram Reel / X video / GIF — developer workflow hook in the terminal; targets the HN/dev audience who found Jot on June 16

shots.visual: Terminal open, cursor after 'git commit -m "', empty — blank stare at the prompt
Keystrokes: 'fix bu' starts to appear; dev stops, deletes it; presses ⌥ Space; Dynamic Island pill appears under Mac notch
Pill pulses with audio waveform; developer speaks the full commit message aloud naturally
Full 13-word commit fills the terminal prompt; pill vanishes; git log scrolls showing the clean entry

drafts.text:
Bad commit messages aren't laziness. They're a typing-friction problem.

When writing hurts, you write less. So commit messages become "fix bug" instead of what actually happened.

I built Jot for exactly this. Press ⌥ Space, speak your commit, it lands in the terminal. 4 seconds. Full context.

Your future self will understand the git log.

What does your commit history look like at 11pm on a Friday?

#macOS #indiedev #buildinpublic
drafts.note: Attach commit-before-after.svg (export as 1080×1080 PNG). Do NOT put the link in the post body — LinkedIn downranks posts with external links. Paste the utm_url as the FIRST COMMENT immediately after posting. The closing question is the engagement trigger — devs who've written 'fix bug' at 11pm will feel this.

drafts.text:
Bad commit messages aren't laziness — they're typing friction.

⌥ Space → speak → "fix null deref when audio device disconnects mid-session" → 4 sec. Free Mac dictation. On-device.
drafts.note: Attach commit-before-after.svg (export as PNG). No link in post body — post the utm_url as the first reply immediately after posting. ~168 characters, within 200 limit.

drafts.text:
I started dictating my git commit messages — my git log is now actually readable (I made this)

For years my commit messages were terrible. Not because I didn't care — because typing a good one at the end of a long session felt like extra work. So they all became 'fix bug' or 'wip' or 'update'.

I started using Jot (the app I built) to dictate them instead. The workflow:
1. Reach the commit step in the terminal
2. Press ⌥ Space
3. Speak the actual thing that happened: "fix null pointer exception when audio device disconnects mid-session on M-series Macs"
4. It pastes directly into the terminal prompt

Total time: about 4 seconds. The commit message is now 13 words instead of 2.

Three things I noticed:
- I write more context when I speak it. The friction of typing made me compress everything.
- Future code reviews are faster — the commit message actually explains the why.
- I now speak PR descriptions and code comments the same way. Same friction problem everywhere.

Jot runs on the Apple Neural Engine — no cloud, no audio leaves the Mac, works offline. Free, open source, Apple Silicon only, macOS 14+.

(I built this — disclosure)

Link in comments.
